zh:
「瓜拉尼神话」不是一部书，也不是一支民族的单声叙述。姆比亚、阿帕波库瓦、派塔维特拉、阿瓦等社群横跨巴拉圭、巴西、阿根廷与玻利维亚，各自保存创世圣歌、迁徙记忆与森林伦理。莱昂·卡多甘在二十世纪中叶记录的《存在之根》让姆比亚长者的「美丽词语」进入文字：Ñamandu在未有地、未有天之时由自身微光中显现，先立言语，再展开世界。纸面只是仪式之声的余烬，不能代替祈祷屋里由人亲口传出的名字。

本馆也收录巴拉圭民间最著名的波姆贝罗、贾西·亚特雷与库鲁皮，但把它们与姆比亚圣歌清楚分层。所谓陶与克拉娜的「七个怪子」主要经诗人纳西索·科尔曼1929年的瓜拉尼语长诗《我们的祖先》定型；那是一部植根民间材料的现代文学综合，不应倒写成所有瓜拉尼人在殖民前共同信奉的古经。圣歌的诸父守护火焰、烟雾、雷雨与灵魂之言，近代怪谈则在午睡、林缘与夜路上巡行。两层传统彼此照映：一个追问世界如何从言语中生长，另一个警告人类，森林从未成为只属于人的领地。
en:
“Guaraní mythology” is neither one book nor the single voice of one people. Mbya, Apapocuva, Paĩ Tavyterã, Ava, and other communities across Paraguay, Brazil, Argentina, and Bolivia keep distinct sacred songs, migration memories, and ethics of the forest. León Cadogan’s mid-twentieth-century Ayvu Rapyta, The Foundation of Human Language, carried the Mbya elders’ “beautiful words” into print: Ñamandu manifests from his own faint radiance before earth or sky, establishes sacred speech, and only then unfolds the world. The page holds an ember of ritual sound; it cannot replace the names spoken inside the prayer house.

This atlas also includes Paraguay’s celebrated Pombero, Jasy Jatere, and Kurupi, while keeping them distinct from Mbya sacred song. The popular cycle of Tau and Kerana’s seven monstrous children was fixed above all by Narciso Colmán’s 1929 Guaraní poem Ñande Ypy Kuéra. It is a modern literary synthesis rooted in folklore, not a lost scripture once shared unchanged by every Guaraní community. The divine fathers of the sacred songs tend flame, mist, thunder, and the soul’s word; the later legendary beings patrol siesta, forest edge, and night road. The two layers reflect one another. One asks how a world grows from language; the other warns that the forest has never become a country belonging only to humankind.
